narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 54% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as schools prioritize admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 25, well below the typical 36.7 average, signaling to the model a significant risk in legal reasoning and exam performance compared to successful peers. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which is slightly above the dataset average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward passing by demonstrating solid academic capability. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—again around the middle but below the average 5.53—provided a milder positive influence, suggesting steady but not standout performance relative to classmates. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status (common among 93% of applicants), played lesser roles. While your profile shows strengths in GPA and consistency, the LSAT's dominant negative impact outweighed them, placing you below the typical successful applicant who combines higher test scores with similar academics. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many with comparable backgrounds thrive with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your overall academic decile through additional coursework might help too. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore prep programs tailored for bar success. With focused effort, you can reapply stronger next cycle.
